conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions about your circumstances.

First, do you have any children that you're responsible for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do. I have a child – Alex, who is 18 years old and lives with me at home.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. 

To help determine eligibility for Alex, I need to ask: Is Alex currently in full-time non-advanced education or approved training?

(This is important because for children aged 16-19, they must be in qualifying education or training for you to be eligible for Child Benefit.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex is still in college doing A-levels.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. That's helpful information.

Next, I need to ask: Are you the person primarily responsible for Alex? In other words, does Alex live with you, and are you the main person looking after them?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives with me and I'm the one looking after them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for confirming that.

Now I need to ask about your residency status: Are you currently living in the UK, and do you have the right to reside in the UK (for example, are you a UK citizen, or do you have settled status, indefinite leave to remain, or another qualifying immigration status)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK – I'm based in Manchester.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you. And just to confirm the second part of my question: Do you have the right to reside in the UK? For example, are you a UK citizen, or do you have settled status, indefinite leave to remain, or another qualifying immigration status?

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, thank you for confirming that.

One final question: Are you or your partner (if you have one) subject to the High Income Child Benefit Charge? This applies if either of you has an individual income over £60,000 per year. 

(Note: This doesn't affect your eligibility to *receive* Child Benefit, but if your income is over £60,000, you may need to pay some or all of it back through your tax return.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, my income is below that threshold.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great news! **You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

Here's why:
- **Alex meets the age and education requirements**: At 18 years old and studying A-levels (full-time non-advanced education), Alex qualifies as a young person in approved education
- **You have primary responsibility**: Alex lives with you and you're the main person looking after them
- **You meet residency requirements**: You're a UK citizen living in the UK
- **No High Income Child Benefit Charge applies**: Your income is below the £60,000 threshold

You can apply for Child Benefit if you haven't already done so. The benefit will continue until Alex turns 20, or until they finish their A-levels or leave approved education or training, whichever comes first.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 18 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 18 and in approved education
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

circumstances: Child is 18 and in approved education
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 18 years old and in full-time non-advanced education (A-levels at college), which qualifies as approved education for Child Benefit purposes for young people aged 16-19. The claimant is primarily responsible for Alex, who lives with them. The claimant is a UK citizen living in the UK (Manchester), meeting the residency requirements. The claimant's income is below £60,000, so the High Income Child Benefit Charge does not apply. All eligibility criteria are met for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex. Alex meets the age and education requirements (18 years old in full-time non-advanced education), the claimant has primary responsibility for Alex who lives with them, and the claimant meets the UK residency requirements as a UK citizen. The income is below the High Income Child Benefit Charge threshold.

The claimant's income is below £60,000, so the High Income Child Benefit Charge does not apply. All eligibility criteria are met for Alex.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex. Alex meets the age and education requirements (18 years old in full-time non-advanced education), the claimant has primary responsibility for Alex who lives with them, and the claimant meets the UK residency requirements as a UK citizen. The income is below the High Income Child Benefit Charge threshold.
